20: makarioi hoi ptochoi, hoti hymetera estin he basileia tou theou — blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God — Luke's beatitudes are in the second person (you, not they as in Matthew) and unqualified (poor without spirit; hungry without for righteousness). Luke's social realism presents poverty and hunger as the literal conditions of Jesus's audience; the kingdom reversal is specifically material as well as spiritual. The four beatitudes are balanced by four corresponding woes (6:24-26), creating the sharpest contrast structure in the Synoptic tradition.
1: en sabbatō deuteroproto — on a Sabbath called “second-first.” The textual problem is acknowledged: deuteroprotos (literally “second-first”) is a hapax legomenon absent from many manuscripts; Origen and Chrysostom knew it; the UBS text brackets it with a C rating. Several interpretations have been proposed: the first Sabbath after Passover, the second Sabbath of a new year counting from Nisan, or the second day of unleavened bread. No solution is fully satisfying; the reading may be a scribal gloss that entered the text. The translation “One Sabbath” wisely avoids specifying.
2: ti poieite ho ouk exestin tois sabbasin — why are you doing what is unlawful on the Sabbath? The Pharisees’ question cites the rabbinic ruling that plucking grain in a field = reaping (m. Shabbat 7:2) and rubbing ears = threshing. The disciples are not stealing; gleaning rights were legally guaranteed to the poor (Lev 19:9-10; Deut 23:25). The dispute is about the category of Sabbath labor, not property. Jesus does not dispute the principle but disputes the application by citing a Torah precedent.
3: oude touto anegnote ho epoiesen Dauid — have you never read what David did? The appeal to the David-precedent (1 Sam 21:1-6) is a rabbinic-style argument from the greater to the lesser: if David’s need justified eating the bread of the Presence (restricted to priests by Lev 24:9), then the disciples’ need justifies gleaning grain on the Sabbath. The argument also implicitly identifies Jesus with David — he who has the authority to define what is lawful.
4: tous artous tēs protheseōs — the bread of the Presence. The shewbread (Exod 25:30; Lev 24:5-9) was the consecrated loaves set before YHWH on the table in the holy place, replaced each Sabbath and eaten only by the priests. David’s eating of them with his companions was technically an irregular priestly action — it was justified by necessity and the priest’s judgment. Luke’s version (unlike Mark 2:26) does not name Abiathar (who is chronologically problematic), quietly avoiding an historical difficulty in the tradition.
5: kyrios estin tou sabbatou ho huios tou anthropou — the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath. Kyrios tou sabbatou (Lord of the Sabbath) — the conclusion is a Christological claim, not merely a principle about human need. The Son of Man title (Aram. bar enasha / Dan 7:13-14) carries the “given authority, glory and sovereign power” of the Danielic figure; to say this figure is Lord of the Sabbath is to claim that he has the authority to define Sabbath observance — the authority of the institution’s Creator.
6: ēn ēi anēr ekei kai hē cheir autou hē dexia ēn xēra — a man was there whose right hand was withered. The specification of the right hand (Luke only; Mark and Matthew say simply “withered hand”) may be Luke’s medical precision (xēra = dry, atrophied, as in medical texts), or it may emphasize the functional disability: the right hand is the working hand; its withering renders the man unable to labor. The healing restores not just physical function but social capacity and livelihood.
7: paretērounto de autou hoi grammateis kai hoi pharisaioi ei en tō sabbatō therapeuei — the scribes and Pharisees were watching him closely to see if he would heal on the Sabbath. Paretērounto (were watching alongside, closely observing) is the same verb used for watching a city gate for an enemy (Acts 9:24); the surveillance is hostile. The rabbinic ruling permitted healing on the Sabbath only in life-threatening cases (m. Yoma 8:6); a withered hand was not life-threatening. Their question is not whether he will heal but whether he will do it on the Sabbath.
8: autos de ēidei tous dialogismous autōn kai eipen tō andri tō xēran echonti tēn cheira egeire kai stēthi eis to meson — he knew their thoughts and said to the man with the withered hand: Get up and stand in front of everyone. Jesus’s knowledge of unspoken thoughts (dialogismous) recurs in Luke (5:22; 9:47; 11:17) as a marker of divine perceptiveness. The public staging of the healing — “stand in the middle” — transforms a potential private healing into a deliberate confrontation with the objectors. He creates the conflict rather than avoiding it.
9: exestin tō sabbatō agathon poiēsai ē kakopoiēsai, psychēn sōsai ē apolysai — which is lawful on the Sabbath: to do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy it? The question restructures the debate from what is prohibited to what is required: the Sabbath is not merely a day for abstaining from labor but a day that belongs to YHWH, who is the God who saves. The antithesis “save life or destroy it” is sharper in Luke than in Mark (where “kill” is the verb). The implication: those who refuse to heal when they can are choosing to destroy life by inaction.
10: eksteinon tēn cheira sou kai epoiēsen — stretch out your hand, and he did so. The healing is accomplished by word alone — no physical touch, no anointing, no clay. Apokatestathē hē cheir autou (his hand was completely restored) — the prefix apo- intensifies the restoration: not merely healed but restored to its former whole state. The instant and complete restoration challenges any claim that healing-work violates the Sabbath; there is no manual labor here, only divine command and its fulfillment.
11: autoi de eplēsthēsan anoias — they were filled with madness. Anoia (madness, senselessness, lack of understanding) is a strong word — the scribes and Pharisees’ response to the undeniable healing is not reflection but rage. Dielaloyn pros allēlous ti an poiēsaien tō Iēsou (they discussed together what they might do to Jesus) — the subjunctive poiēsaien expresses deliberation; the conspiracy against Jesus begins here in Luke. The Sabbath healing has exposed the opposition of those who should have welcomed the divine mercy.
12: exēlthen eis to oros proseuxasthai kai ēn dianyktereuōn en tē proseuchē tou theou — he went out to a mountainside to pray, and he spent the night in prayer to God. Dianyktereuōn (spending the night, passing the night) is a NT hapax — only Luke records this detail. Luke’s Jesus prays at all the decisive moments of the Gospel: before the choosing of the Twelve (here), at his baptism (3:21), before Peter’s confession (9:18), at the Transfiguration (9:28-29), in Gethsemane (22:41-44). The appointment of the apostles is preceded by a night of prayer: human discernment submits to divine guidance.
13: hous kai apostolous onomasen — whom he also named apostles. The title apostolos (sent one, commissioned representative) distinguishes these twelve from the larger group of disciples who also followed Jesus. Luke’s use of apostle is more technically defined than Mark’s: the Twelve are the authoritative witnesses who will testify to the resurrection and lead the early community (Acts 1:15-26; the requirement of having witnessed from the beginning). The naming is an act of commissioning, not merely labeling.
14: Simōna hon kai onomasen Petron — Simon, whom he also named Peter. The naming of Simon as Peter (Petros = rock) is noted by Luke without explanation; the reader is assumed to know the significance. The list of the Twelve follows Markan order with slight variations; Andreas (Andrew) is Simon’s brother; Iacōbus and Iōannēs (James and John) are the Zebedee brothers; Philippos (Philip) and Bartholomaios (Bartholomew, possibly = Nathanael in John) complete the first six.
15: Matthaion kai Thoman, Iakōbon ton tou Alphaiou kai Simōna ton kaloumenon Zēlōtēn — Matthew, Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot. Zēlōtēs (Zealot) may denote membership in the revolutionary group that advocated violent resistance to Rome, or it may simply mean “the zealous one” in a general sense. Luke also records Matthew in this list, the same figure as Levi of 5:27-29. The Twelve include tax collectors and a Zealot — representatives of opposite ends of Jewish political loyalty — united by their calling to follow Jesus.
16: Ioudan Iakōbou kai Ioudan Iscariōth, hos egeneto prodotēs — Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor. The final entry in the list is marked by prodotēs (traitor, betrayer — a strong word; the same term used for those who hand over fellow believers in Acts 7:52). Egeneto (became) rather than “was” suggests Judas’s treachery is not his original state but a tragic development. Iscariot is most likely “man from Kerioth” (a Judean town), making Judas possibly the only non-Galilean among the Twelve.
17: kai stēnai epi topou pedinou kai ochlos polys mathētōn autou kai plēthos poly tou laou — he came down with them and stood on a level place, with a large crowd of his disciples and a great multitude of people. The “level place” (topou pedinou, plain, flat place) has generated a harmonization question with Matthew’s “mountain” (Matt 5:1); these may be different occasions, or Luke may intend a level area on a mountainside. The audience is explicitly double: mathētōn (disciples, the larger following) and laou (the people, including the geographical range from Judea, Jerusalem, and the Phoenician coastal cities).
18: hoi enochloumenoi hypo pneumatōn akathartōn etherapeuonto — those troubled by unclean spirits were cured. The summary verse covers the two healings: those with diseases (nosōn) and those with unclean spirits (pneumata akatharta). Etherapeunto (were being healed, imperfect = continuous action) alongside the explicit exorcisms indicates that the kingdom’s arrival manifests as liberation from both physical illness and spiritual oppression — Luke’s consistent double-healing motif.
19: pas ho ochlos ezētoun haptesthai autou, hoti dynamis par autou exērchetō kai iato pantas — all the crowd were trying to touch him, because power was coming out from him and healing everyone. Dynamis par autou exērchetō (power was going out from him) — dynamis (power, divine energy) is Luke’s characteristic term for the operative force in Jesus’s healings (5:17; 8:46; Acts 10:38). The imperfect exērchetō (kept going out) portrays a continuous flow of healing power; Jesus is not merely performing isolated miracles but radiating the kingdom’s restorative power to all who seek it.
21: makarioi hoi peinōntes nyn, hoti chortasthēsesthe — blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Peinōntes (hungering, literally starving) has no qualifier “for righteousness” as in Matthew (Matt 5:6); the literal hunger of the poor is blessed. Chortasthēsesthe (you will be filled, satisfied — the word used for animals fed to contentment, Ezek 16:49 LXX) is the divine passive: God will satisfy the hungry with good things (cf. Magnificat 1:53). The second beatitude (klaiō weep/mourn) and its reversal (gelasete you will laugh) are also second-person and literal.
22: makarioi este hotan misēsōsin hymas hoi anthrōpoi — blessed are you when people hate you. The fourth beatitude is the longest and most social: it names four escalating actions — hate (misēsōsin), exclude (aphorizōsin, excommunicate), insult (oneidisōsin, reproach), reject as evil (ekbalōsin to onoma hymon hōs ponēron, cast out your name as evil). The cause is “on account of the Son of Man” — the connection between the disciples’ suffering and Jesus’s own rejection is made explicit. Identification with Jesus brings the treatment Jesus receives.
23: chairete en ekeinē tē hēmera kai skirtēsate — rejoice in that day and leap for joy. Skirtēsate (leap, jump for joy) is the word for John’s prenatal leap (1:41, 44) and for the calf released from the stall in Mal 4:2 LXX — it is the extravagant, uninhibited joy of liberation. Hoti gar ho misthos hymon polys en tō ouranō (for your reward is great in heaven) — the eschatological framing: present suffering has a corresponding heavenly weight of glory. The prophets-as-precedent clause (kata tauta gar epoioun tois prophētais) identifies the disciples with the OT prophetic succession.
24: plēn ouai hymin tois plousiois, hoti apechete tēn paraklēsin hymōn — but woe to you who are rich, for you have already received your comfort. The four woes (vv.24-26) are Luke’s unique contribution; Matthew has no parallels. Ouai (woe) is the prophetic word of judgment and grief (as in Isa 5:8-23; Amos 5:18). Apechete (you have received in full, a commercial term for receipts — “paid in full”) — the rich have already cashed in their comfort; no future comfort awaits them. The economic reversal of the Magnificat (1:52-53) is here applied as direct address.
25: ouai hymin hoi empeplēsmenoi nyn, hoti peinas ete — woe to you who are well fed now, for you will go hungry. The future tense of the woes (peinas ete you will hunger; penthēsete kai klausete you will mourn and weep) is eschatological: the reversal is not immediate but certain. The structural parallelism with the beatitudes is exact: the well-fed now will hunger; those who hunger now will be filled. The pairing condemns not wealth or satiety in themselves but the false security they create — the assumption that present comfort is permanent.
26: ouai hotan hymas kalōs eipōsin pantes hoi anthrōpoi — woe to you when everyone speaks well of you. The fourth woe closes the chiasm with the fourth beatitude: the blessed are socially rejected; the condemned are socially praised. Kata ta auta gar epoioun tois pseudoprophētais (for that is how their ancestors treated the false prophets) — universal social approval was the mark of the false prophet (Jer 5:31; Mic 2:11), not the true. Popularity is a warning sign, not a validation.
27: agapate tous echthroys hymōn, kalōs poieite tois misousin hymas — love your enemies, do good to those who hate you. Agapate (love, the command form of agapaō — not eros or philia but the willed, active disposition of seeking the other’s good regardless of their response) directed at enemies is the most radical element of the Sermon. The enemies are defined in relational terms: those who hate (misousin), those who curse (katarōmenois), those who mistreat (epēreazontōn). The love commanded is not an emotion but a practice: do good, bless, pray.
28: eulogeite tous katarōmenous hymas, proseuchesthe peri tōn epēreazontōn hymas — bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you. The four pairs form a chiastic pattern of increasing intimacy of response: love (general disposition), do good (practical action), bless (spoken contrary to curse), pray (internalized self-offering for the other). Eulogeite (bless) is the spoken counterpart to the received curse; proseuchesthe (pray) is the deepest: interceding for those who actively mistreat is the completion of love.
29: tō typtonti se epi tēn siagona parechete kai tēn allēn — if someone strikes you on the cheek, turn to them the other also. The cheek-slap in the honor-shame culture was a blow of contempt rather than assault; to offer the other cheek is not passive submission but active refusal to be defined by the attacker’s framework. To himation (outer garment, the more valuable) and to chiton (inner tunic) — the coat/shirt reversal: if someone takes the more valuable outer cloak, give also the inner. The legal background (Exod 22:26) makes this a commentary on debt-recovery law: give beyond what can legally be taken.
30: panti tō aitounti se didou kai apo tou ainontos ta sa mē apaiteite — give to everyone who asks you, and if anyone takes what belongs to you, do not demand it back. The principle is unlimited generosity rather than calculated reciprocity. Apaiteite (demand back, exact return) — the word for legally demanding repayment; the disciples are called to release claims rather than enforce them. The verse operates against the honor economy that requires debts to be balanced and insults to be avenged.
31: kai kathōs thelete hina poiōsin hymin hoi anthrōpoi, poieite autois homoiōs — do to others as you would have them do to you. The Golden Rule appears across many traditions (Rabbi Hillel’s negative form: “What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbor”, Shabbat 31a; Tobit 4:15). Jesus’s formulation is positive and proactive rather than negative and restraining. Luke places it after the enemy-love commands, giving it a universalizing function: the standard for treatment of enemies is the same as the standard for all human relations.
32: kai ei agapate tous agapōntas hymas, poia hymin charis estin — if you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? Charis here means “credit, merit, deserving” (not grace in the theological sense) — what distinguishes-to-your-advantage does this give you? The three examples (love-for-love, v.32; good-for-good, v.33; lend-to-repay, v.34) are all practices of calculated reciprocity that even “sinners” (hamartōloi) perform. The disciples are called to something qualitatively beyond the social norm.
33: kai gar hoi hamartōloi tous agapōntas autous agapōsin — even sinners love those who love them. The repeated appeal to “sinners” as the baseline of ordinary human reciprocity sets a rhetorical contrast: the disciples are not called to outperform the Torah-observant but to exceed the practices of those outside the covenant entirely. Hamartōloi (sinners) in the Synoptic context typically means those outside the social/religious norms of Jewish piety — tax collectors, Gentiles, the disreputable.
34: kai ean danizēte par hōn elpizete labein, poia hymin charis estin — and if you lend to those from whom you expect repayment, what credit is that to you? The lending-with-expectation example moves into the economic sphere: ancient lending was often social capital exchange rather than commercial transaction. Lending to those who will repay preserves the honor network; lending to those who cannot repay is economic gift. Kai hamartōloi hamartōlois danizousin hina apolabōsin ta isa (even sinners lend to sinners expecting to receive back the same amount) — pure reciprocity is the sinners’ economic logic.
35: plēn agapate tous echthroys hymon kai agathopoieite kai danizete mēden apelpizontes — love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them without expecting to get anything back. The climactic command repeats and intensifies v.27; the three verbs (love, do good, lend without expectation) are the practical content of the love commanded. Hoti autos chrēstos estin epi tous acharistous kai ponērous — because God is chrēstos (kind, good) to the ungrateful and wicked. The divine character is the ground of the ethical demand: imitate God’s indiscriminate kindness. Chrēstos is a Greek wordplay on Christos (Christ), possibly intentional in Hellenistic Christian ears.
36: ginesthe oiktirmones kathōs kai ho patēr hymon oiktirmōn estin — be merciful, just as your Father is merciful. Oiktirmōn (merciful, compassionate) is the LXX rendering of Hebrew rachum (the womb-compassion of God, Ps 103:8; Exod 34:6 — “the LORD, the LORD, the compassionate and gracious God”). Matthew’s parallel reads “be perfect as your Father is perfect” (Matt 5:48); Luke’s “merciful” is both a translation variant and a thematic emphasis: Luke’s Gospel is organized around divine mercy (eleos) and human compassion (oiktirmos) as the central kingdom qualities.
37: kai mē krinete kai ou mē krithēte; kai mē katadikasete kai ou mē katadikasthēte; apolyete kai apolythēsesthe — do not judge and you will not be judged; do not condemn and you will not be condemned; forgive and you will be forgiven. Three paired commands with divine-passive responses. The sequence escalates: not judging (withholding negative evaluation) → not condemning (withholding legal-moral verdict) → releasing (active release of claims/offenses). Apolyete (release, forgive) is the same root as aphesis (forgiveness/release), Luke’s programmatic gospel term. The passive responses are divine: God judges, condemns, and releases in corresponding proportion.
38: metron kalon pepiesmenon sesaleumenon hyperekchynnomenon dōsousin eis ton kolpon hymōn — a good measure, pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be poured into your lap. The four participial clauses describe the excess of the divine measure given in return for generous giving: pepiesmenon (pressed down, compacted to fit more), sesaleumenon (shaken together to settle), hyperekchynnomenon (overflowing, running over the edge). Eis ton kolpon (into the lap/fold) — the fold of a robe used to carry grain; the image is of market abundance. Hō gar metrō metreite antimethrēthēsetai hymin — the measure principle applies both to human generosity and to divine judgment.
39: mēti dynatai typhlos typhlon hodēgein; ouchi amphoteroi eis bothynon pesountai — can the blind lead the blind? Will they not both fall into a pit? The parable of the blind guide is applied contextually to the scribes and Pharisees as blind guides (cf. Matt 23:16, 24) who cannot lead others in the way of the kingdom they cannot see. Luke’s context immediately links this to the teacher-student principle (v.40): the disciples cannot exceed their teacher’s sight. The warning applies to the disciples too: if they follow blind guides rather than Jesus, they will share the guide’s fate.
40: ouk estin mathētēs hyper ton didaskalon; katērtismenos de pas estai hōs ho didaskalos autou — the student is not above the teacher; but everyone who is fully trained will be like their teacher. Katērtismenos (fully trained, completely equipped — the verb used for mending fishing nets, Matt 4:21; for the body assembled by God, Heb 10:5) — the goal of discipleship is not to exceed the teacher but to become like him. The implication: only a disciple of Jesus, who is not blind, can follow a guide who sees. The teacher defines the ceiling of the student’s achievement.
41: ti de blepeis to karphos to en tō ophthalmō tou adelphou sou — why do you look at the speck of sawdust in your brother’s eye? Karphos (speck, small piece of straw or wood chip) and dokos (plank, beam) form an absurdist contrast: the one criticizing perceives a tiny defect in the other while being oblivious to a structural beam in their own eye. The humor is deliberately exaggerated; the point is the selective blindness of self-righteousness. The address adelphou (brother) situates the critique within the covenant community — those who should know better are most prone to this blindness.
42: hypokritē, ekbale prōton tēn dokon ek tou ophthalmou sou — you hypocrite, first take the plank out of your own eye. Hypokritēs (hypocrite, actor — one who performs a role different from their reality) is the only use of this term in Luke; Matthew uses it far more frequently. The command sequence is important: first (prōton) self-examination and correction, then (tote) correction of the other. The verse does not forbid correction; it requires the corrector to be in a state of self-examination first. The cleared eye is the condition of seeing clearly enough to help another.
43: ou gar estin dendron kalon poioun karpon sapron, oude palin dendron sapron poioun karpon kalon — a good tree does not bear bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit. The tree-fruit principle is a creation-order consistency argument: the nature of the tree determines the nature of the fruit; there is no arbitrary disconnection between internal character and external action. The principle is applied to human moral character (vv.44-45): what a person produces consistently reveals what they are, not merely what they choose to do in isolated moments.
44: hekastos gar dendron ek tou idiou karpou ginōsketai — each tree is recognized by its own fruit. Ginōsketai (is known, recognized, identified) — the fruit is the epistemological key to the tree’s identity. The examples (figs from thornbushes, grapes from briers) invoke Isa 5:2,4 (the vineyard that produced wild grapes — the symbol of Israel’s unfaithfulness) and the Synoptic tradition of demanding fruits of repentance (3:8-9). The conclusion is diagnostic: you recognize the tree by what it consistently produces.
45: ho agathos anthrōpos ek tou agathou thēsaurou tēs kardias propherei to agathon — a good man brings good things out of the good stored up in his heart. Thēsauros kardias (treasure of the heart) — the heart is the storage vault of moral character; what is stored there (agathos good or ponēros evil) is what comes out in speech and action. Ek gar perisseumaros kardias lalei to stoma autou (from the overflow of the heart the mouth speaks) — the heart is not merely the emotions but the deep moral center of the person (OT usage: Jer 17:9; Ps 51:10). Speech is involuntary testimony to the heart’s actual condition.
46: ti de me kaleite: kyrie kyrie, kai ou poieite ha legō — why do you call me Lord, Lord and do not do what I say? The doubled kyrie kyrie (Lord, Lord) is liturgical address — a formulaic reverence in worship. Jesus’s question challenges the gap between verbal acknowledgment and practical obedience. The title Kyrios (Lord) is the LXX rendering of YHWH; to call Jesus Lord without obeying him is to use the divine name as an incantation while ignoring the divine command. The parable that follows defines what hearing-and-obeying looks like concretely.
47: pas ho erchomenos pros me kai akouōn mou tōn logōn kai poiōn autous — everyone who comes to me and hears my words and puts them into practice. The three participial phrases define the obedient disciple: coming (erchomenos — direction of life, orientation toward Jesus), hearing (akouōn — active reception of his teaching), and doing (poiōn — practical embodiment). Luke’s version of the parable (vv.47-49) differs from Matthew’s (Matt 7:24-27) in the construction details: Luke specifies digging deep (eskapse kai ebathynen), while Matthew mentions rock. Luke’s emphasis is on the effort of deep foundation-laying.
48: homoios estin anthrōpō oikodomounti oikian hos eskapse kai ebathynen kai ethēken themelion epi tēn petran — they are like a man building a house who dug down deep and laid the foundation on rock. Eskapse kai ebathynen (dug and deepened) — Luke’s unique detail: the obedient disciple labors to reach bedrock; the process is effortful, not obvious. The Palestinian setting: houses were sometimes built on alluvial soil; a flood (plēmyrēs, a rising of water, an inundation) could undermine the foundations. The flood-house-foundation triad invokes Noah’s ark and the testing of structures by crisis. Dia to kalōs oikodomēsthai (because it was well built) — Luke’s explanation emphasizes craftsmanship; Matthew emphasizes rock as the foundation material.
49: ho de akousas kai mē poiēsas homoios estin anthrōpō oikodomēsanti oikian epi tēn gēn chōris themelion — but the one who hears and does not put it into practice is like a man who built a house on the ground without a foundation. Chōris themelion (without a foundation, lacking a foundation) — the structural deficiency is not poor materials but the complete absence of foundational support. Hē rhēxis tēs oikias ekeinēs egeneto megalē (the ruin of that house was great) — Luke’s intensified conclusion: the collapse is complete (cf. Matthew’s “great was its fall”). The stakes of hearing-without-doing are not minor adjustment but total catastrophic failure in the testing crisis.
